book_claims.claim: Dried apricots suggested as a dessert in the Longevity Diet; source of vitamin A (63 mcg RAE per 10 halves dried, 25% DV) as beta-carotene
book_claims.mechanism: Dried fruit source of provitamin A (beta-carotene); beta-carotene is a fat-soluble antioxidant carotenoid that converts to retinol in the intestine

supplementary_research.finding: Apricot consumption (PMID 22789423) is associated with reduced markers of oxidative stress and improved antioxidant status; beta-carotene and chlorogenic acid are primary active compounds
Dried apricots have a moderate glycemic index (~31 dried) and high fiber content (PMID 16820341); their polyphenol content supports gut microbiome diversity and reduced postprandial glucose response

Apricots are a good source of potassium, beta-carotene, and dietary fiber; dried form concentrates nutrients but also sugar; pairing with nuts (as the Longevity Diet recommends) blunts glycemic impact

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Fat-soluble; absorption significantly enhanced by co-ingested fat (e.g., nuts or olive oil)
Highly bioavailable; supports blood pressure regulation and cardiovascular health
Soluble and insoluble fiber; supports gut microbiome and slows sugar absorption
Absorbed in small intestine and colon; anti-inflammatory and antioxidant
Pairing with vitamin C significantly improves absorption

synergies.description: Classic Longevity Diet dessert pairing; walnuts' fat enhances beta-carotene absorption from apricots; protein and fat buffer apricot's sugar
Fat from olive oil dramatically increases bioavailability of apricot's beta-carotene (provitamin A)
Pairing apricots with a vitamin C source (e.g., citrus) enhances non-heme iron absorption from the dried fruit
